briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for May 2, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Technological Innovation
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are underway to transition towards a carbon-neutral society. Korea Gas Corporation (KOGAS) is simultaneously pursuing carbon neutrality practices and sharing through a step donation campaign [1], and Daegu Metropolitan City is spreading a culture of carbon neutrality practices through the activities of its youth supporters [4]. Notably, in Jeju, an AX smart farm utilizing AI technology is drawing attention for ushering in an era of carbon neutrality in the livestock sector through the smart operation of pig farms [3]. Furthermore, Dr. Kim Dong-sun, a principal researcher at the Korea Institute of Oriental Medicine, highlights the industrial potential of underutilized native plants, demonstrating the importance of sustainable resource utilization [5]. These efforts represent significant steps contributing to the achievement of domestic carbon neutrality goals.

sections.title: ⚠️ Intensifying Seasonal Impacts of Climate Change
sections.content: Globally, the direct impacts of climate change are manifesting in seasonal patterns, raising concerns. Specifically, rapid fluctuations in spring temperatures are occurring, a phenomenon exacerbated by climate change [6, 10]. Concurrently, observations suggest that winters are becoming shorter [12], and the potential disappearance of April showers has been mentioned, further increasing concerns about drought [15, 16]. These drastic seasonal shifts are expected to have widespread impacts on ecosystems and human life.

sections.title: 🔴 Climate Crisis, Deepening Social Inequality
sections.content: Warnings are emerging that the climate crisis is exacerbating social inequality and placing a greater burden on vulnerable populations. Indigenous peoples are directly hit by climate change but receive little funding to combat it [11], and the head of Christian Aid emphasized that the climate crisis is fundamentally altering the fight against global poverty [13]. Low-income immigrant communities in Houston, USA, are living in survival mode, facing complex crises including immigration crackdowns, chemical spills, and massive floods [17]. Furthermore, biodiversity is also under threat, as exemplified by Mendoza's Pehuenche frog facing climate change and invasive species threats [14]. This once again highlights the importance of climate justice.

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Response and Adaptation Strategies
sections.content: The international community is exploring various strategies to respond to climate change. Japan is moving to preemptively secure the next-generation eco-friendly shipbuilding sector, aiming to lead the green transition of future marine transportation [2]. Meanwhile, discussions are taking place in Michigan on how to adapt dams to climate change, demonstrating the importance of infrastructure adaptation [9]. Climate awareness is also spreading in individuals' lives, with the introduction of a new funeral method called 'green death' for activists concerned about climate change and carbon footprints [8]. However, political differences in views on climate change still exist, as exemplified by Lee Zeldin criticizing the Democratic Party's 'pessimistic' climate change views after a heated House hearing [7].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the severe impacts of climate change, alongside the simultaneous domestic and international efforts underway to overcome them. This is a time when an integrated approach for a sustainable future becomes even more crucial. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Discovering the Path to Industrialization of Native Plants from 'Gaetkkachisuyeom'
articles.description: Kim Dong-sun, Principal Researcher at the Korea Institute of Oriental Medicine, previously introduced the Korean mock orange (Philadelphus schrenkii) in a column titled 'Forgotten Native Plants, Reasons to Bring Them Back to the Table.' The Korean mock orange served not just as a rediscovery of an edible resource, but as an example demonstrating the industrial potential of our native plants. However, many underutilized native plants in Korea still lack sufficient scientific verification and application. Climate change and...
